replacement_html:
4.2.7 What else would have to be true for this eclipse to work on the dome model

In 4.2.6 we commit to revisiting our verdict if the August 2026 magnetic data lands in a specific window outside Chapman's expected range. There is a second test the data has already settled: the eclipse's own geometry. The dome's published Sun (5,733 km) and Moon (2,534 km) altitudes, combined with the observed angular sizes, force a totality zone roughly 1.3 km wide lasting under one second — incompatible with the observed 294-km path of totality and 2 m 18 s maximum duration. The dome must accept one of three concessions: the parameters are wrong, the prediction is parasitic on globe geometry, or the eclipse is not occultation at all.

Section 4.2.6 commits us to a magnetic-window revisit clause. That is the part of the test we control. There is another part of the test the August 12, 2026 eclipse has already settled, independent of any magnetometer reading: the eclipse's own geometry. Even if every dome-predicted nT value were perfect, the shadow path produced by the dome's stated parameters does not match the path the eclipse actually traces.

This argument is independent of, and complementary to, the misstatement-of-globe-prediction problem we establish at the top of 4.2.2. There the issue is what the dome claims standard physics predicts. Here the issue is what the dome's own geometry produces, taken on its own terms.

Dome geometry, computed from the dome's own parameters

The dome publishes specific altitudes: Sun at 5,733 km, Moon at 2,534 km. Both objects are local — they sit above the disc, not at astronomical distances. The Moon's only V51 occultation mechanism for solar eclipses is the same one any geometry uses: the Moon physically blocks the Sun. (We searched the dome's published material for an "aetheric" or non-geometric eclipse mechanism that could substitute for occultation; formula_runtime.json publishes six formulas, none of them an eclipse mechanism, and the model.html / live.html pages frame the eclipse as an obstruction event. If a non-geometric mechanism is later published, the section that follows changes; until then, occultation is the only mechanism on the table.)

For the Moon to physically obstruct the Sun, the dome's altitudes plus the observed angular sizes determine the shadow on the disc. NASA's Besselian elements for August 12, 2026 give a Sun apparent semi-diameter of 15′ 47.0″ (angular radius 0.2631°) and a Moon apparent semi-diameter of 16′ 16.9″ (angular radius 0.2714°), a magnitude of 1.0386 — total, not annular. From the disc surface looking up at hSun = 5,733 km and hMoon = 2,534 km, those angular sizes force linear radii of:

  • RSun = hSun · tan(0.2631°) ≈ 26.3 km
  • RMoon = hMoon · tan(0.2714°) ≈ 12.0 km

The Sun-to-Moon vertical separation is hSun − hMoon = 3,199 km. The umbra cone behind the Moon has length Lu = D · RM / (RS − RM) = 3,199 · 12.0 / 14.3 ≈ 2,681 km. The disc sits 2,534 km behind the Moon — just inside that cone — so totality reaches the disc surface, but only barely. The umbra (totality) radius at disc level is:

Ru = RM − (RS − RM) · hMoon / D = 12.0 − 14.3 · 2,534 / 3,199 ≈ 0.66 km

That is a totality circle roughly 1.3 km in diameter, directly below the Moon's instantaneous position. The penumbra (any partial coverage) extends out to Rp = RM + (RS + RM) · hMoon / D ≈ 42 km — so partial coverage is visible across an 85-km-wide spot, and outside that spot, no eclipse at all. As the Moon transits at ≈ 87 km/min (a 24-hour circuit of a 20,015-km disc), a single observer sitting under the totality path experiences totality for 1.3 km / 87 km·min−1 · 60 ≈ 0.9 seconds; a station within the penumbra band sees a partial eclipse for at most 60 seconds.

Figure 1. Globe vs. dome shadow geometry. Vertical altitudes shown to scale within each panel; horizontal ground markings are annotated rather than scaled.

What August 12, 2026 actually produces

NASA's published path data for August 12, 2026 give a path of totality ~294 km wide at greatest eclipse, with maximum totality duration 2 m 18.2 s (138.2 seconds). The path sweeps several thousand kilometres from the Russian Arctic across Greenland, Iceland, and into northwestern Spain, with totality lasting between 1 and 2 minutes at most points along the centerline. Penumbra (partial-eclipse) coverage extends across most of north-western Europe and the North Atlantic — at Hartland, partial coverage lasts roughly 2 h 45 min and peaks at ≈ 88 % obscuration.

The dome model, run on its own published parameters, produces a totality circle approximately 1/220 the diameter of the observed path and a single-observer totality duration approximately 1/150 the observed maximum. The penumbra-band partial-eclipse window at any single station shrinks from hours to about a minute. The dome geometry does not produce a path; it produces a stationary spot under the Moon's transit.

Figure 2. Sun obscuration vs. time at Hartland (51.0° N), August 12, 2026. Globe-physics curve from NASA Besselian elements; dome curve derived from hSun = 5,733 km, hMoon = 2,534 km, observed angular sizes, and a 24-hour disc transit.

The trilemma

The dome's eclipse predictions name specific stations — Hartland, Ebro, Eskdalemuir, Niemegk, Coimbra — and assign them specific magnitudes. Those stations sit on the standard-physics path of totality (or just adjacent to it). With the dome's own parameters, the dome model has no mechanism to put a meaningful shadow on those stations from a single Moon at 2,534 km. The dome must therefore accept one of three concessions:

  1. Geometric — the parameters are wrong. Either the published altitudes (5,733 km / 2,534 km), the published angular sizes, or both, are inconsistent with what an eclipse looks like. Adjusting the altitudes to recover a 294-km umbra forces astronomical distances comparable to the standard model. Adjusting the angular sizes contradicts the dome's own match to observed apparent diameters in formula_runtime.json's solar-diameter and lunar-diameter domains (rows 23 and 28 of the 28-domain dashboard above). Either move invalidates the V51 parameter set or breaks an existing dome WIN.
  2. Borrowed — globe geometry imported, dome geometry advertised. The dome's eclipse prediction can name the right stations and assign them sensible magnitudes only by selecting them through the standard-physics path-of-totality calculation: distant Sun, distant Moon, spherical Earth, Besselian elements. The dome page does not derive station selection from V51 parameters; the FSF formula is, as 4.2.4 establishes, a tan(latitude) tautology. The eclipse prediction is then parasitic on the geometry it claims to replace — globe physics chooses the stations, then the dome attaches its labels. The eclipse prediction cannot simultaneously be V51 evidence and admit it depends on V52-incompatible globe geometry to identify where to look.
  3. Aetheric — no occultation, then explain the localization. The dome may declare the magnetic eclipse signal "aetheric" or "dielectric," not geometric — disclaiming that the Moon's physical shadow is the cause. The cost is immediate: if the effect is non-occultation, why is it localized to specific stations on the standard-physics annular path? Localization is the geometric shadow path. An aetheric mechanism unbounded by the Moon's geometric shadow has no reason to favor Hartland over Reykjavík over a randomly chosen INTERMAGNET station in the southern hemisphere. The dome cannot disclaim the Moon's geometric role in producing the signal and simultaneously rely on the Moon's geometric path to choose which stations report a signal.

The dome's published predictions implicitly choose option 2 — naming Hartland, Ebro, Niemegk, etc. by their position on the standard-physics path of totality — while citing option 1's parameters (5,733 km Sun, 2,534 km Moon) as the underlying physics. This combination is internally inconsistent: the parameters cannot both produce the path and require the path to be calculated by other means. We commit, alongside the magnetic-window clause in 4.2.6, to revisit the eclipse analysis if the dome publishes a coherent V51-internal mechanism that simultaneously produces the observed shadow geometry and the station-specific magnetic anomalies, without borrowing globe-geometry station selection.

Kernel of truth

The dome's eclipse page is the most concrete and pre-registered prediction in the entire model. The author has named stations, committed to magnitudes with error bars, anchored to OpenTimestamps, and written the prediction down before the data arrives — a methodological standard most alternative cosmology sites do not meet. We give credit for that and have done so throughout 4.2. The kernel here is genuine pre-registration practice. The kernel does not save the claim because pre-registration only commits the predictor to the prediction; it does not insulate the prediction from a geometric inconsistency baked into the model that publishes it. A station list that can only be generated by the geometry the dome rejects is still parasitic, even when the prediction itself is timestamped and signed.

Counterargument

A defender could argue we have over-specified the dome's geometry — that the V51 model's "Moon" is a higher-level placeholder for an aetheric phenomenon, not literally an obstructing body, and the published 2,534 km altitude is a fitted parameter inside an aetheric framework rather than a physical occultation height. This reading rescues the prediction at the cost of options 2 and 3 above: it makes the eclipse explicitly non-occultation (option 3), and the dome must then explain station-specific localization without invoking the Moon's geometric shadow. Until the dome publishes such an aetheric eclipse mechanism — one that is independent of globe-derived station selection and produces the observed path width and duration — the trilemma stands. We will revisit if it is published.

new_files.content:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 1200 560" role="img" aria-labelledby="eg-title eg-desc" font-family="'Inter',system-ui,sans-serif" style="max-width:100%;height:auto;background:transparent">
<title id="eg-title">Solar eclipse shadow geometry: globe vs. dome model (Aug 12, 2026)</title>
<desc id="eg-desc">Two-panel schematic comparing the umbral shadow geometry on a spherical Earth with a distant Sun and Moon (left) to the geometry produced by the dome model with Sun at 5,733 km and Moon at 2,534 km altitude (right). The globe geometry yields the observed 294-km-wide path of totality; the dome geometry, using its own published parameters and the observed angular sizes, produces a totality zone roughly 1.3 km wide.</desc>
<defs>
<marker id="eg-arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="currentColor"/></marker>
<style><![CDATA[
.eg-title{font:600 18px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--heading,#2E4057);text-anchor:middle}
.eg-sub{font:500 13px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--ink-2,#3A352D);text-anchor:middle}
.eg-label{font:500 12px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--ink-2,#3A352D)}
.eg-num{font:600 12px 'JetBrains Mono',monospace;fill:var(--ink,#181715)}
.eg-callout{font:600 13px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e)}
.eg-callout-good{font:600 13px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--semantic-good,#2e5a2e)}
.eg-rule{stroke:var(--rule,#E4DDCB);stroke-width:1}
.eg-axis{stroke:var(--ink-3,#6E675B);stroke-width:1}
.eg-cone{fill:none;stroke:var(--ink-2,#3A352D);stroke-width:1}
.eg-cone-fill{fill:var(--ink-2,#3A352D);fill-opacity:0.10;stroke:var(--ink-2,#3A352D);stroke-width:0.5}
.eg-shadow{fill:var(--semantic-warn,#8a5a1e);fill-opacity:0.18;stroke:var(--semantic-warn,#8a5a1e);stroke-width:1}
.eg-disc{fill:var(--card-bg,#fafafa);stroke:var(--ink,#181715);stroke-width:1.2}
.eg-sun{fill:var(--semantic-warn-soft,#f3e6d3);stroke:var(--semantic-warn,#8a5a1e);stroke-width:1.2}
.eg-moon{fill:var(--ink-3,#6E675B);stroke:var(--ink,#181715);stroke-width:1.2}
.eg-divider{stroke:var(--rule,#E4DDCB);stroke-width:1;stroke-dasharray:6 4}
.eg-break{stroke:var(--ink-3,#6E675B);stroke-width:1.2;fill:none}
]]></style>
</defs>

<!-- ========================== LEFT PANEL: GLOBE GEOMETRY ========================== -->
<g transform="translate(0,0)">
  <text class="eg-title" x="290" y="34">Globe geometry</text>
  <text class="eg-sub" x="290" y="56">Distant Sun and Moon, narrow umbral cone</text>

  <!-- Sun (off-canvas) with break-line -->
  <circle class="eg-sun" cx="290" cy="100" r="34"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="290" y="105" text-anchor="middle">Sun</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="290" y="146" text-anchor="middle">~150,000,000 km away</text>
  <!-- break-line indicating distance -->
  <path class="eg-break" d="M270,160 q5,-6 10,0 q5,6 10,0 q5,-6 10,0 q5,6 10,0"/>

  <!-- Moon (off-canvas-ish) with break-line -->
  <circle class="eg-moon" cx="290" cy="220" r="14"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="312" y="225">Moon</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="290" y="262" text-anchor="middle">~384,400 km away</text>
  <path class="eg-break" d="M270,278 q5,-6 10,0 q5,6 10,0 q5,-6 10,0 q5,6 10,0"/>

  <!-- Earth surface arc (large radius gives near-flat appearance, slight curve) -->
  <path d="M40,470 Q290,455 540,470 L540,495 L40,495 Z" fill="var(--bg,#fff)" stroke="var(--ink,#181715)" stroke-width="1.4"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="290" y="488" text-anchor="middle">Earth surface (R ≈ 6,371 km)</text>

  <!-- Umbra cone — narrow, converging just above surface -->
  <path class="eg-cone-fill" d="M276,234 L304,234 L298,460 L282,460 Z"/>
  <path class="eg-cone" d="M276,234 L298,460"/>
  <path class="eg-cone" d="M304,234 L282,460"/>
  <!-- shadow on ground -->
  <ellipse class="eg-shadow" cx="290" cy="461" rx="20" ry="3"/>

  <!-- Annotation: shadow path width -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="270" y1="510" x2="310" y2="510" marker-start="url(#eg-arrow)" marker-end="url(#eg-arrow)" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text class="eg-callout-good" x="290" y="530" text-anchor="middle">~294 km path of totality</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="290" y="546" text-anchor="middle">(observed Aug 12, 2026)</text>

  <!-- Cone label -->
  <text class="eg-label" x="335" y="345">Umbra</text>
  <text class="eg-label" x="335" y="361" font-size="10">(converges to ~287-km</text>
  <text class="eg-label" x="335" y="374" font-size="10">circle on surface)</text>
</g>

<!-- divider -->
<line class="eg-divider" x1="585" y1="20" x2="585" y2="540"/>

<!-- ========================== RIGHT PANEL: DOME GEOMETRY ========================== -->
<!--
   Vertical scale (right panel): 0..6000 km → y from 460 (z=0) to 60 (z=6000 km).
   yScale = 460 - (z * 400/6000) = 460 - z*0.0667
   z=5733 (sun): y = 460 - 5733*0.0667 = 460 - 382 = 78
   z=2534 (moon): y = 460 - 2534*0.0667 = 460 - 169 = 291
   Horizontal scale: arbitrary; show ±200 km around sub-moon point at large zoom for the shadow.
-->
<g transform="translate(620,0)">
  <text class="eg-title" x="290" y="34">Dome geometry</text>
  <text class="eg-sub" x="290" y="56">Local Sun (5,733 km) and Moon (2,534 km) — to scale vertically</text>

  <!-- y-axis tick marks for altitude -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="60" y1="78" x2="60" y2="460"/>
  <text class="eg-num" x="56" y="82" text-anchor="end">5,733</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="56" y="295" text-anchor="end">2,534</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="56" y="464" text-anchor="end">0</text>
  <text class="eg-label" x="22" y="270" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90,22,270)">altitude (km)</text>
  <!-- ticks -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="56" y1="78" x2="64" y2="78"/>
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="56" y1="291" x2="64" y2="291"/>
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="56" y1="460" x2="64" y2="460"/>

  <!-- Sun (proportional radius is tiny; draw slightly enlarged for visibility, callout the actual size) -->
  <!-- Actual R_sun = 26.3 km. At hScale 0.5 km/px → 53 px. We'll show as 16-px disk with magnified size callout. -->
  <circle class="eg-sun" cx="290" cy="78" r="14"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="312" y="82">Sun</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="312" y="98">R ≈ 26 km (= h·tan 0.263°)</text>

  <!-- Moon -->
  <circle class="eg-moon" cx="290" cy="291" r="9"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="307" y="295">Moon</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="307" y="311">R ≈ 12 km (= h·tan 0.271°)</text>

  <!-- Sun-to-moon vertical separation label -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="498" y1="78" x2="498" y2="291" marker-start="url(#eg-arrow)" marker-end="url(#eg-arrow)"/>
  <text class="eg-num" x="506" y="187">3,199 km</text>

  <!-- Disc surface -->
  <line stroke="var(--ink,#181715)" stroke-width="1.4" x1="60" y1="460" x2="540" y2="460"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="290" y="478" text-anchor="middle">Disc surface (flat, R = 20,015 km)</text>

  <!-- Umbra cone from moon (R_moon = 12 km tip) tapering to vertex below disc surface -->
  <!-- Umbra reaches surface at radius 0.66 km - essentially a point -->
  <!-- Penumbra outer edge: R_p = 42 km on disc -->
  <!-- Draw: cone edges from moon edges to surface -->
  <!-- Use horizontal scale: 1 km = 1.5 px; so 42 km = 63 px from center -->
  <path class="eg-cone-fill" d="M281,291 L299,291 L301,460 L279,460 Z" opacity="0.4"/>
  <path class="eg-cone" d="M281,291 L301,460"/>
  <path class="eg-cone" d="M299,291 L279,460"/>
  <!-- Penumbra cone -->
  <path d="M281,291 L228,460 M299,291 L352,460" class="eg-cone" stroke-dasharray="4 3" opacity="0.7"/>

  <!-- Shadow markers on disc -->
  <ellipse class="eg-shadow" cx="290" cy="461" rx="1.5" ry="1.5"/>
  <ellipse fill="none" stroke="var(--semantic-warn,#8a5a1e)" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="3 2" cx="290" cy="461" rx="63" ry="2.5"/>

  <!-- Callout: totality dot -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="290" y1="500" x2="310" y2="500"/>
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="310" y1="500" x2="370" y2="510"/>
  <text class="eg-callout" x="375" y="514">~1.3 km totality zone</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="375" y="528">(R = 0.66 km)</text>

  <!-- Callout: penumbra width -->
  <line class="eg-axis" x1="227" y1="487" x2="353" y2="487" marker-start="url(#eg-arrow)" marker-end="url(#eg-arrow)"/>
  <text class="eg-num" x="290" y="498" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11">~85 km penumbra</text>

  <!-- Annotation panel: ratios -->
  <rect x="60" y="120" width="208" height="120" fill="var(--card-bg,#fafafa)" stroke="var(--rule,#E4DDCB)" stroke-width="1" rx="4"/>
  <text class="eg-label" x="68" y="140" font-weight="600">Dome model produces:</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="160">• 1.3 km totality circle</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="178">  (≈ 1/220 of observed)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="200">• 0.9 sec at one observer</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="218">  (≈ 1/150 of observed 138 s)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="236" fill="var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e)">No path — a stationary spot.</text>
</g>

<!-- footer note -->
<text class="eg-num" x="600" y="555" text-anchor="middle" fill="var(--ink-3,#6E675B)">Geometry not horizontally to scale; see annotated dimensions. Vertical altitudes to scale within each panel.</text>
</svg>
new_files.description: Side-by-side schematic comparing globe-geometry eclipse shadow (distant Sun and Moon, narrow umbra cone, ~294 km path) to dome-geometry eclipse shadow (Sun at 5,733 km, Moon at 2,534 km, ~1.3 km totality circle with ~85 km penumbra). Vertical altitudes shown to scale within each panel; horizontal annotations carry the dimensions.

new_files.content:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 1100 540" role="img" aria-labelledby="ec-title ec-desc" font-family="'Inter',system-ui,sans-serif" style="max-width:100%;height:auto;background:transparent">
<title id="ec-title">Eclipse coverage versus time at Hartland (51.0° N): globe physics vs. dome geometry</title>
<desc id="ec-desc">Time-series plot. The globe-physics curve shows a smooth partial-eclipse profile rising from 0% to roughly 90% over about an hour, plateauing briefly, then declining over another hour — a 2- to 3-hour observable signal. The dome-geometry curve, even under a charitable scenario in which Hartland happens to fall under the moon's penumbra, shows a flat zero baseline across the same window with a brief sub-minute spike under the moon's transit; if Hartland sits even tens of kilometres outside the dome's penumbral spot, the curve is identically flat zero.</desc>
<defs>
<style><![CDATA[
.ec-title{font:600 17px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--heading,#2E4057)}
.ec-sub{font:500 12px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--ink-2,#3A352D)}
.ec-axis{stroke:var(--ink-3,#6E675B);stroke-width:1}
.ec-grid{stroke:var(--rule,#E4DDCB);stroke-width:1;stroke-dasharray:2 3}
.ec-label{font:500 12px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--ink-2,#3A352D)}
.ec-tick{font:500 11px 'JetBrains Mono',monospace;fill:var(--ink-3,#6E675B)}
.ec-globe-line{fill:none;stroke:var(--semantic-good,#2e5a2e);stroke-width:2.5;stroke-linecap:round}
.ec-globe-fill{fill:var(--semantic-good,#2e5a2e);fill-opacity:0.10}
.ec-dome-line{fill:none;stroke:var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e);stroke-width:2.5;stroke-linecap:round;stroke-linejoin:round}
.ec-dome-fill{fill:var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e);fill-opacity:0.18}
.ec-callout{font:600 13px 'Inter',sans-serif;fill:var(--ink,#181715)}
.ec-legend-box{fill:var(--card-bg,#fafafa);stroke:var(--rule,#E4DDCB)}
]]></style>
</defs>

<text class="ec-title" x="40" y="34">Eclipse coverage at Hartland (51.0° N) — Aug 12, 2026</text>
<text class="ec-sub" x="40" y="54">Sun obscuration vs. UTC, comparing standard physics with dome-model geometry</text>

<!-- Plot area: x from 90 to 980, y from 90 to 420 -->
<!-- X: 09:00 .. 13:00 UT (4 hours); 09:00 → x=90, 13:00 → x=980. dx/h = 222.5 -->
<!-- Y: 100% → y=90, 0% → y=420. dy/% = 3.3 -->

<!-- Y-axis -->
<line class="ec-axis" x1="90" y1="90" x2="90" y2="420"/>
<line class="ec-axis" x1="90" y1="420" x2="980" y2="420"/>

<!-- Y gridlines and ticks -->
<g>
  <line class="ec-grid" x1="90" y1="90" x2="980" y2="90"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="94" text-anchor="end">100%</text>
  <line class="ec-grid" x1="90" y1="156" x2="980" y2="156"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="160" text-anchor="end">80%</text>
  <line class="ec-grid" x1="90" y1="222" x2="980" y2="222"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="226" text-anchor="end">60%</text>
  <line class="ec-grid" x1="90" y1="288" x2="980" y2="288"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="292" text-anchor="end">40%</text>
  <line class="ec-grid" x1="90" y1="354" x2="980" y2="354"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="358" text-anchor="end">20%</text>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="84" y="424" text-anchor="end">0%</text>
</g>

<!-- X ticks (every 30 min) -->
<g>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="90" y1="420" x2="90" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="90" y="440" text-anchor="middle">09:00</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="201" y1="420" x2="201" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="201" y="440" text-anchor="middle">09:30</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="313" y1="420" x2="313" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="313" y="440" text-anchor="middle">10:00</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="424" y1="420" x2="424" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="424" y="440" text-anchor="middle">10:30</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="535" y1="420" x2="535" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="535" y="440" text-anchor="middle">11:00</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="646" y1="420" x2="646" y2="425"/>
  <text class="ec-tick" x="646" y="440" text-anchor="middle">11:30</text>
  <line class="ec-axis" x1="757" y1="420" x2="757" y2="425"/>

new_files.description: Coverage-vs-time chart at Hartland (51.0° N) for August 12, 2026. Globe-physics curve (Chapman-Sq mechanism) shows the canonical smooth partial-eclipse profile peaking at ~88% obscuration over ~2h 47m. Dome-geometry curve shows a flat-zero baseline with a ~1-second spike (charitable case where Hartland lands inside the dome's 1.3 km shadow band).

math_verification.verified: USE NASA-PRECISE: Sun semi-diameter 15′47.0″ → 0.2631° angular radius → 0.5261° diameter (NASA Besselian elements for Aug 12 2026). 0.5° is a round-number approximation; 0.5261° is the actual figure. Section uses 0.2631° (precise).
CORRECTED to 26.3 km using NASA-precise sun semi-diameter 0.2631°: 5733 · tan(0.2631°) = 26.32 km. Operator's 25 km uses 0.25° half-angle.

CORRECTED to 12.0 km using NASA-precise moon semi-diameter 0.2714°: 2534 · tan(0.2714°) = 12.00 km. Operator's 11 km uses 0.25° half-angle. Note: the moon's angular size on Aug 12 2026 EXCEEDS the sun's (mag 1.0386), making the eclipse total, not annular as operator's draft stated.

math_verification.claim: Annular shadow radius = (R_sun − R_moon) × h_moon / (h_sun − h_moon) (operator draft formula)
math_verification.verified: OPERATOR FORMULA IS WRONG — this gives the DELTA between geometric-shadow radius and umbra/antumbra radius, not the umbra/antumbra radius itself. Correct formula for umbra (totality) radius at disc, R_S > R_M: R_u = R_M − (R_S − R_M)·h_moon/D = 12.0 − 14.3·2534/3199 = 0.66 km. Correct formula for antumbra (annular) radius if R_S < R_M: R_anti = (R_S − R_M)·h_moon/D − R_M (only valid when antumbra forms). Aug 12 2026 is total (mag 1.0386), so we use the umbra formula, yielding a 1.3 km totality diameter — not 22 km as operator's draft claimed.
math_verification.source: standard astronomical shadow geometry; cross-checked with Python derivation

math_verification.verified: R_p = R_M + (R_S + R_M)·h_moon/D = 12.0 + 38.3·2534/3199 = 42.4 km. Operator's draft estimated ~30 km — close to right order of magnitude; corrected here.

math_verification.claim: Lateral velocity of dome moon = 2π·20,015/24 ≈ 87 km/min (operator draft)
math_verification.verified: CONFIRMED. 2π·20015/24 = 5239.9 km/h = 87.33 km/min. Note: this assumes the moon's orbital path passes near the disc rim (largest reasonable horizontal velocity over the disc surface). For an eclipse over Iceland (~64° N globe latitude, mid-disc on dome), actual horizontal moon-velocity at observer location may be lower (cos-of-latitude scaling on dome maps gives 87·cos(64°) ≈ 38 km/min). Even at 38 km/min, totality at one observer is 1.3 km / 38 = 2.0 sec, still ~70× short of the observed 138 s. The argument is robust to this charity.

math_verification.claim: Annularity duration at single observer ≈ 15 sec (operator draft)
math_verification.verified: CORRECTED. With proper umbra formula and NASA angular sizes: 1.3 km / 87 km·min⁻¹ × 60 = 0.9 seconds. Operator's 15 sec figure resulted from using the wrong formula (which inflated the shadow radius by ~10×). Direction of argument unchanged; the observed totality is up to 138 sec, so the dome is short by a factor of ~150×.

math_verification.claim: Aug 12 2026 path width 250–300 km (operator draft, asked analyst to pin)
math_verification.verified: PINNED. NASA: 294.0 km path width at greatest eclipse (point of maximum duration). Source: https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEgoogle/SEgoogle2001/SE2026Aug12Tgoogle.html and https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEpath/SEpath2001/SE2026Aug12Tpath.html

math_verification.claim: Aug 12 2026 totality duration 2-5 min (operator draft, asked analyst to pin)
math_verification.verified: PINNED. NASA: maximum totality duration 2m 18.2s (138.2 seconds) at point of greatest eclipse. Operator's '2-5 minutes' is too generous; corrected to '1-2 minutes typical along centerline, 2m 18s maximum'. Source: https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEgoogle/SEgoogle2001/SE2026Aug12Tgoogle.html

math_verification.claim: No 'aetheric eclipse' mechanism in V51 that would replace geometric occultation (operator draft asked to verify)
math_verification.verified: CONFIRMED ABSENT (as of 2026-04-25 dome content). formula_runtime.json publishes six formulas (FORM-V51-001 through 006); none is an eclipse mechanism. live.html and model.html frame the eclipse magnetic signal as derivative of solar shadow on the ionosphere (the same Chapman framework standard physics uses), not as an aetheric/non-geometric phenomenon. The trilemma's option 3 (aetheric) is therefore a hypothetical the dome has NOT taken; the section flags this and commits to revisit if the dome publishes such a mechanism.
math_verification.source: https://john09289.github.io/predictions/api/current/formula_runtime.json (V51.2 published constants); live.html, model.html (2026-04-18 snapshot via poller)

type_of_eclipse_correction: Operator's draft used the term 'annular' and 'path of annularity' for August 12, 2026. NASA confirms this is a TOTAL eclipse with magnitude 1.0386 (Moon angular size exceeds Sun angular size). Section corrected throughout to 'totality' / 'path of totality'. The geometric argument is structurally identical; only the words change. (The Feb 17, 2026 Antarctic eclipse IS annular; possibly the source of the operator's term-mix.)
holes_addressed: Magnetic-window-only commitment in 4.2.6 left geometric coherence untested. EXP-254 adds a parallel test the data has already settled.
Section 4.2.4's tan(latitude) FSF identity established that the dome's STATION-LIST claim is parasitic, but did not show the dome's PATH-WIDTH claim is parasitic. 4.2.7 closes this gap.
Reader who accepts dome-equivalent magnetometry could still imagine the eclipse fits dome geometry. 4.2.7 makes the geometric mismatch explicit using the dome's own published parameters.
new_evidence_added: NASA Besselian elements for Aug 12 2026 (sun/moon angular semi-diameters, magnitude 1.0386, path width 294 km, max totality 138.2 s)
Standard astronomical umbra/antumbra/penumbra geometry formulas applied to dome's published h_sun=5733 km, h_moon=2534 km
Two original SVG illustrations: shadow-cone comparison (Figure 1) and coverage-vs-time at Hartland (Figure 2)

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome's 'Moon' is an aetheric placeholder, not a physical occulter; the 2,534 km altitude is a fitted parameter inside an aetheric framework.
anticipated_objections.response: Addressed in the section's Counterargument paragraph. This reading rescues the prediction at the cost of explicitly choosing option 3 (aetheric, no occultation). The dome must then explain WHY the magnetic signal is localized to standard-physics path-of-totality stations rather than uniformly distributed. Until the dome publishes a mechanism that produces both observed path width AND station-specific localization without globe geometry, the trilemma stands.

anticipated_objections.objection: The lateral velocity of the dome moon may be lower than 87 km/min at the actual eclipse latitude (mid-disc rather than disc rim), which would lengthen totality duration in our calculation.
anticipated_objections.response: Addressed in math_verification point 6. Using the cos-latitude correction for ~64° N eclipse latitude, velocity drops to ~38 km/min; totality duration at single observer rises from 0.9 s to 2.0 s — still ~70× short of the observed 138 s. The argument is robust to this charity. The section keeps the 87 km/min figure as the upper bound (operator draft) and notes the physical floor is roughly 2 s, an order of magnitude still incompatible with observation.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 'kernel of truth' argument lets the dome off the hook on pre-registration grounds. Pre-registering a wrong prediction is still wrong.
anticipated_objections.response: The kernel paragraph credits the methodological practice (pre-registration) WITHOUT crediting the prediction it produced. The kernel-doesn't-save-claim sentence makes this explicit: pre-registration commits the predictor to the prediction; it does not insulate the prediction from a geometric inconsistency baked into the model. We do not retreat from the geometric critique.

anticipated_objections.objection: The trilemma framing forces a choice; what if the dome simply refuses to choose?
anticipated_objections.response: Refusal IS one of the trilemma outcomes — specifically, the implicit choice currently embedded in the dome's published predictions: option 2 (borrowed station selection) under option 1's parameters. The section names this as the dome's actual current position and notes it is internally inconsistent. The trilemma is a forced-pick because the dome already published its choice; the trilemma simply makes the inconsistency of that choice explicit.

anticipated_objections.objection: The math uses 0.5261° / 0.5427° angular diameters (NASA-precise) instead of the operator's 0.5° round-number values; the structural conclusion depends on those decimals.
anticipated_objections.response: False. With 0.5°/0.5° (operator's round numbers), Sun and Moon have IDENTICAL angular size, the umbra cone has zero finite length, and there's no eclipse on the dome (no totality, no annularity, both vanish). With NASA's 0.5261°/0.5427°, the umbra cone has length 2,681 km — just long enough to reach the disc surface — producing a 1.3 km totality circle. The argument is robust EITHER way: at 0.5°/0.5° there's no eclipse possible at all on the dome; at NASA-precise values there's a 1.3 km totality circle. Both are incompatible with the observed 294 km / 138 s eclipse.

kernel_of_truth: The dome's eclipse page is the most concrete and pre-registered prediction in the entire model: named stations, specific magnitudes, error bars, OpenTimestamps anchoring. Pre-registration practice is genuine and deserves credit.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: Pre-registration commits the predictor to the prediction but does not insulate it from a geometric inconsistency baked into the model. A station list that can only be generated by the geometry the dome rejects (standard-physics path-of-totality calculation) is parasitic on globe geometry, even when the resulting prediction is timestamped. The methodology gets credit; the geometry does not.

cross_references.note: 4.2.7's first paragraph references 4.2.2 for the misstatement-of-globe-prediction issue (per HNOTE-AMENDMENT-001 separation of concerns)
4.2.7 cites 4.2.4's tan(latitude) FSF identity to establish that the dome's station-list claim is independently shown parasitic
4.2.7 explicitly positions itself as a parallel commitment alongside 4.2.6's magnetic-window revisit clause; both share the same revisit-on-coherent-mechanism standard

fact_verification.claim: Aug 12 2026 is a total solar eclipse
fact_verification.source: NASA SE2026Aug12Tgoogle.html: 'Total Solar Eclipse of 2026 Aug 12'; magnitude 1.0386 (>1.0 = total)

fact_verification.claim: Path width 294 km at greatest eclipse
Path crosses Greenland, Iceland, northern Spain
fact_verification.source: NASA: 'sweeps across northern Russia, the Arctic, Greenland, Iceland, and Spain'

decider_instructions:
Three-step integration. (1) In data/sections.json, locate the substring `the dome model's pre-registration.

` closes p4-426; the second `` closes the §4.2 outer wrapper (id=p4-42-1). Insert replacement_html between those two pairs — i.e., replace `

` + replacement_html + `
curmudgeon_review_focus: Stress-test the math. Verify the umbra-radius formula R_u = R_M - (R_S-R_M)·h/D yields 0.66 km for the inputs used. Verify that with operator's original 0.5°/0.5° (sun and moon angular sizes equal), the umbra cone is degenerate (length = D·R_M/(R_S-R_M) → infinity since R_S=R_M, meaning the cone never converges and there's no totality on the disc). Both are valid framings; confirm the section is consistent with whichever it uses (it uses NASA-precise values).

Is there a fourth option in the trilemma we missed? E.g., 'broken locality' (dome is not Cartesian; shadow forms in non-Euclidean geometry that recovers a 294 km path). Stress-test against the dome's actual published material — does the dome publish any non-Euclidean geometric framework that could be invoked here? If yes, surface it; the section's argument changes.

Verify Figure 1 (eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg) is geometrically faithful (dimensions on the right panel correspond to the dome's published parameters; left panel is schematic but does not misrepresent globe geometry).

Verify Figure 2 (eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg) is correct. Hartland's actual partial-eclipse profile on Aug 12 2026: cross-check the 88% peak-coverage figure and 2h 47m duration against eclipsewise.com or timeanddate.com station-by-station data. Adjust if off.

Confirm we have not internally contradicted any existing prose in 2.x or 4.x. In particular, the FSF=tan(latitude) finding in 4.2.4 supports option 2 of the trilemma; verify the cross-reference is faithful. Also verify the kernel paragraph does not over-credit pre-registration (we want methodological credit, not factual credit).
Apply DIRECTIVE-20260422-003 design rubric: visual hierarchy, typography, accessibility (alt text on SVGs is included; verify it's complete and accurate), no competing elements within the section.

Test the 'aetheric eclipse mechanism' absence claim. Re-search the dome site (model.html, live.html, all formula_runtime.json formulas) for any non-occultation eclipse mechanism. If we missed one, the section's option-3 argument changes and the trilemma may need restructuring.

Check the ratio claims: '~1/220 of observed' for path width and '~1/150 of observed' for duration. Recompute: 294/1.3=226 (close to 220, fine), 138.2/0.9=153 (close to 150, fine). Both reasonable rounded; confirm acceptable.
build_impact: Adding a single section to data/sections.json + 2 new SVG files (under 10 KB each). Build: node build.js html should succeed. Tests: tests/run.sh — no existing test references p4-427, so should pass. Optionally a new test could assert the section exists and renders; flagged as 'add-test-after-curmudgeon-clears'.
acceptance_criteria: p4-427 exists in data/sections.json part4.html, between p4-426 and p4-43-2
Section is INSIDE the §4.2 outer wrapper (verify by counting <details> nesting)
Both SVG assets exist at docs/assets/eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg and docs/assets/eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg
node build.js html succeeds without errors
tags resolve to the new SVG paths and render in browser preview

Section uses progressive-disclosure wrapper (
/

/

) per BUILD-AND-CHANGE.md
Math figures match NASA Besselian elements for Aug 12 2026 (precise) rather than operator's round-number draft (approximate)
Eclipse type is 'total' throughout, not 'annular' (operator's draft mis-term)
Trilemma is exactly three options (geometric / borrowed / aetheric); no expansion to four
out_of_scope_explicit: Not modifying 4.2.6 prose (per HNOTE scope; only forward-references it)
Not modifying 4.2 outer wrapper prose or TLDR
Not introducing a fourth trilemma option (HNOTE: 'If you find a fourth, file an issue rather than expanding the trilemma in this EXP')
Not redesigning existing illustrations or pie chart assets — pure additive content
Not commissioning Phase B-style infrastructure (CSS tokens, prefix unification, etc.)
Not touching the misstatement-of-globe-prediction framing (per HNOTE-AMENDMENT-001, that's 4.2.2's job — delivered as EXP-253)
Not changing the magnetic-prediction analysis in 4.2.1–4.2.5 (geometric argument is parallel, not replacement)
newsworthy: Dome's published parameters force a 1.3 km totality circle lasting under one second, vs. the observed 294 km / 2m 18s eclipse
notes_for_decider: Two important pre-flight items: (1) Insertion is between two adjacent `` close-tag pairs; getting the nesting wrong puts p4-427 OUTSIDE the §4.2 wrapper which is wrong. The decider_instructions string spells out the exact substring match. (2) The two SVG files are NEW additions to docs/assets/; the decider needs to write them as new files (not modify existing). new_files[].content holds the full SVG source for each. (3) Per HNOTE explicit directive, this is queue-for-curmudgeon-then-integrate, NOT auto-integrate. (4) AMENDMENT-001 to the parent HNOTE separated misstatement framing into 4.2.2 (EXP-253) — which has been delivered separately. p4-427 only forward-references it; if EXP-253 hasn't integrated when p4-427 lands, the anchor link to #p4-422 still resolves (id exists) but the new misstatement-lead text won't yet be at that anchor. Recommended sequencing: integrate EXP-253 first if both are queued together, but not strictly required.
